segments.s112-g73: This is a sequence of coordinated predicates describing the subject (omitted, 3rd person). بخفت is a simple past (he slept). بر آسود is a poetic adjectival predicate ('was at rest') where بر + آسود indicates the state 'upon/at rest' and is coordinated with the previous verb by و. نگشاد is a negative form (ن + گشاد, past/primitive stem of گشودن) meaning 'did not open'; لب (lit. 'lip') is the object/thing not opened (i.e. he did not speak).
segments.s112-g74: This clause continues with an implied subject and gives what was done with the horse Rakhsh. چمان here functions adverbially/idiomatically (rendered 'thus' / 'so') and is paired with چران, a verbal/nominal form meaning 'grazing' or 'the act of grazing'; together they indicate that Rakhsh was left to graze/browse. رخش is the horse (direct object/affected noun), and تا نیم‌شب is a temporal phrase ('until midnight') modifying the preceding action. The compact, archaic order omits an overt subject and coordinates actions succinctly, typical of epic verse.
segments.s112-g1: یکی راه پیش آمدش ناگزیر is built as a compact clause: یکی ('a certain') modifies راه ('path/way'); پیش آمدش is a past action with the clitic -ش marking 'to him' (آمد + ش = 'came to him'); ناگزیر is an adjective following the clause meaning 'inevitable' or 'compelled' and describes the situation. Word order is typical older/poetic Persian: determiner + noun + verbal phrase + predicate adjective.
segments.s112-g2: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past-continuous form (همی- prefix gives ongoing aspect: 'was going'); بایست here functions as a modal/semantically 'had to / must (stop or stand)' rather than a simple intransitive 'stand'; بر introduces the following phrase ('upon/at'); خیره is a participial/adjectival form 'staring/astonished' used adverbially; خیر is a noun ('goodness/pleasant sight') describing what he stared at. The segment therefore says he was moving but was compelled to stop and stare at the good/wondrous sight.
segments.s112-g3: پی اسپ places پی ('after, following') before اسپ ('horse') to mean 'following the horse' or 'behind the horse'; و is the simple conjunction 'and'; گویا is a parenthetical adverb 'it seemed / as if' qualifying the next phrase; زبان سوار is a genitival/possessive-like sequence 'the rider's tongue' (literally 'tongue of the rider'), here referring to the rider's speech or ability to speak.
segments.s112-g4: ز گرما و از تشنگی شد ز کار is an idiomatic sequence: ز / از both mean 'from' and mark cause ('from the heat and from the thirst'); شد is the past of شدن 'became' (here 'came to be' or 'was rendered'); ز کار literally 'from work/function' = 'out of action' or 'incapacitated'. Put together it says he (or his tongue) became exhausted/incapacitated because of heat and thirst.
segments.s112-g5: پیاده شد literally 'became on-foot' and functions as 'he dismounted' (پیاده used adjectivally/verbally); از اسپ = 'from the horse'; و is 'and'; ژوپین is the gambeson or padded jacket; به دست ('to/into hand') here means 'in his hand' — i.e. he took or held the gambeson in his hand. The verb شد is the past auxiliary forming 'became/on-foot'.
segments.s112-g6: همی‌رفت is the imperfective/past-continuous ('was going, continued to go'); پویان here is an adjectival/participial use meaning 'moving' or 'on the move' (it qualifies his manner of going); به کردار = 'in the manner/behaviour of' (کردار = conduct, used adverbially with به); مست = 'drunk/intoxicated' (here describing his gait or behavior figuratively). Together: he went on, moving in the manner of a drunk.
segments.s112-g7: همی‌جست is the imperfective/past-continuous form ('kept seeking' — همی- marking ongoing action); بر چاره جستن combines بر (here marking the object/purpose) with چاره جستن ('to seek a remedy/solution'), so the clause says he kept searching for a remedy/solution; رهی functions as an indefinite/relational noun derived from 'راه' meaning 'a way' or 'a course' — poetically used here to indicate seeking a way out.
segments.s112-g8: سوی آسمان کرد روی آنگهی is a small clause where سوی ('toward') governs آسمان ('the sky'); کرد is the past of کردن used here causatively/lexically to mean 'made' or 'turned' in combination with روی ('face'); آنگهی = 'then/thereupon' places the action in sequence. The idiom کرد روی = 'turned his face' so the whole segment reads 'then he turned his face toward the sky.'
segments.s112-g57: This is a compact past-clause: بشستش is the past tense verb 'he washed him/it' with the object-suffix -ش (him/it). همه تن precedes the verb as the direct object in typical Persian order (determiner + noun = 'the entire body'). بران آب پاک is a prepositional/nominal phrase modifying the washing: بران (= 'that/thereon') + آب پاک ('pure water'), where بران points back to something mentioned earlier ('that water') and functions like a demonstrative determiner.
segments.s112-g58: به کردار خورشید شد تابناک is a result/clause introduced by شد 'became' (past of شدن). به کردار is an adverbial prepositional phrase meaning 'in the manner of' (به used adverbially), خورشید is the noun being compared to, and تابناک is the predicate adjective 'radiant' placed after شد as is normal when شدن functions as a copula ('became radiant').
segments.s112-g59: چو سیراب شد is a temporal subordinate clause: چو 'when' + سیراب شد 'became sated/quenched' (past of شدن). ساز نخچیر کرد follows as the main action: ساز here means 'device/gear' and نخچیر 'game/prey' (i.e. hunting/spoil); کرد is the simple past 'he made/prepared' — together meaning he prepared his hunting equipment/arranged for the spoil.
segments.s112-g60: کمر بست is a two-word verbal phrase: کمر 'waist' + بست (past of بستن) 'he bound/tied' = 'he girded his waist'. The coordinating و 'and' links the second verb phrase ترکش پر از تیر کرد where ترک + ش is the broken-up form of ترکـش 'his quiver' (ش = possessive) and پر از تیر 'full of arrows' is the object phrase; کرد is the past auxiliary 'he made/filled.' Note: the token 'ترک' as listed does not include 'quiver' in the provided senses, so the sense used here is not among the supplied options.
segments.s112-g61: بیفگند is a past tense verb 'he threw/cast' (bi‑prefix + past). گوری is the direct object 'a grave/tomb' or more generally 'a pit' placed after the verb (poetic inversion is common); چو پیل ژیان is a simile introduced by چو 'like/as' modifying the throwing — پیل 'elephant' and ژیان 'living/life' together give 'like a living elephant' or 'as an elephant in size/vitality.' Grammatically, چو + noun phrase is a standard comparative/simile clause.
segments.s112-g62: جدا کرد is a verbal clause meaning 'he separated/off removed' (جدا as adverb/adjective 'apart' + کرد past 'did'). ازو 'from it/him' marks the source; چرم پای و میان is the direct object phrase ('the hide/skin of the legs and the middle/belly') with و joining پای ('leg/foot') and میان ('middle/midriff'). Word order is verb + source + object (poetic freedom in ordering is common).
segments.s112-g63: چو خورشید introduces a simile ('as/like the sun'). تیز آتشی is a noun phrase where تیز 'sharp/keen' modifies آتشی 'fiery/fire' (آتشی is an adjectival/attributive form meaning 'fiery' or 'a fire'), and برفروخت is the past tense verb 'kindled/ignited' (here used transitively: he kindled a blazing fire). The order places the simile marker before the verb for poetic emphasis.
segments.s112-g64: This clause strings three verbal/locative elements: برآورد (here past of برآوردن, 'he lifted/removed/brought up') is the main verb; ز آب 'from the water' indicates the source; اندر آتش 'into/in the fire' marks the destination or location where the next verb acts; بسوخت (past passive/intransitive form of سوختن) means 'it burned' or 'was burned.' Grammatically, a sequence of past verbs describes consecutive actions: he took it from the water and (then) it burned in the fire. Note: the provided senses for برآورد did not include 'lift/bring up,' so the correct sense is not among the supplied options.
segments.s112-g65: بپردخت is an archaic/poetic past form (third person) whose exact lexical senses in the provided list don't fit perfectly here; syntactically it functions as the main past verb 'it leapt/fell/was thrown'. ز آتش is a prepositional phrase 'from the fire' (ز = 'from' before the noun). به خوردن گرفت is a light-verb construction where به + infinitive (خوردن) + گرفت (= 'took'/'began') means 'began to be eaten' or 'took to being eaten' — گرفت here conveys the inceptive sense 'took to / began'.
segments.s112-g66: به خاک استخوانش is a goal/locative phrase 'into/onto the earth his bones' (به = 'towards/into', خاک = 'earth/soil', استخوانش = 'his bones'). سپردن گرفت repeats the light-verb pattern: the infinitive سپردن 'to consign/hand over' plus گرفت ('began/took') yields 'began to be consigned' or 'his bones began to be handed over (to the earth)'.
segments.s112-g67: سوی چشمه روشن is a prepositional phrase 'toward the bright spring' (سوی = 'toward(s)', چشمه روشن = 'bright/clear spring'). آمد is the past tense main verb 'came'. بر آب is a locative 'to/onto the water' (بر here indicates movement to the water's edge/onto the water); the verb آمد follows the direction phrase, a common word order in classical Persian.
segments.s112-g68: چو سیراب شد is a temporal clause 'when/after he became sated' (چو = 'when', سیراب شد = 'was/ became sated'). کرد آهنگ خواب is a verbal phrase in which کرد ('made/did') plus آهنگ ('intention/aim') means 'made toward / turned his aim to' and خواب = 'sleep' gives 'he turned toward sleep' or 'he made for sleep.' Here کرد functions causatively/lexically as 'set himself toward.'
segments.s112-g69: تهمتن is a proper name or epithet in the vocative/subject slot (here treated as the speaker's name/title). به رخش is the indirect object 'to Rakhsh' (به = 'to/for', رخش = the steed's name). سراینده is an appositive epithet 'the singer/songster' describing Rakhsh; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke' placed at the end. Note: گفت is the reporting verb; in the provided sense list none exactly match the verbal sense 'said', so no index is given for that item.
segments.s112-g70: که introduces the quoted admonition ('that' / 'that is, ...'). با کس مکوش is an admonitory negative imperative: با کس = 'with anyone' and مکوش = negative imperative 'do not strive/contend' (مکوش = مکن + کوش). و مشو نیز جفت continues with و = 'and', مشو = negative imperative 'do not become', نیز = 'also/likewise', and جفت here means 'mate/companion' — so the sense is 'do not strive with anyone, nor become a mate/companion either.'
segments.s112-g71: اگر دشمن آید is a conditional clause 'if an enemy comes' (اگر = 'if', دشمن آید = 'an enemy comes'). سوی من بپوی is an imperative clause where سوی + object indicates direction 'toward me' and بپوی is an imperative 'seek/pursue/make for' — together: 'make for me' or 'come to me.' The verb is an aoristic/imperative form instructing movement toward the speaker.
segments.s112-g72: تو is the vocative/explicit subject 'you'. با دیو و شیران is a prepositional phrase 'with demons and lions' (با = 'with'). مشو جنگجوی is a negative imperative sequence: مشو = 'do not become' and جنگجوی = 'a warrior/fighter' — 'do not become a warrior with/against demons and lions' (i.e., do not engage in battle against such foes yourself). Word order places negation before the predicate noun.
segments.s112-g25: ازان رفتن میش اندیشه خاست is a compact nominal phrase + verb: ازان رفتن ('from that going/movement') modifies اندیشه ('a thought'), and خاست (archaic past 'arose') is the main verb. Poetic word order places the modifying phrase before the head noun (اندیشه) and the verb خاست follows it; خاست is an older form equivalent to برخاست/بروز آمد (meaning 'arose' or 'came into being').
segments.s112-g26: به‌دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'she said in her heart / to herself' (speaking inwardly). که introduces a subordinate indirect question clause; آبشخور این کجاست literally orders as 'water-source this where-is' with the copula ست (enclitic) at the end—typical poetic order—so the clause reads 'where is this watering-place?'.
segments.s112-g27: همانا is an emphatic particle 'verily / truly'. که here functions as a subordinator 'that' linking to the following clause. بخشایش کردگار is a genitive phrase 'the generosity/forgiveness of the Creator' (poetic style omits the ezafe), so بخشایش is modified by کردگار.
segments.s112-g28: فراز آمده‌ست is an idiomatic predicate: فراز 'on high / above' + آمده‌ست (present perfect 'has come') = 'has risen on high'. اندرین روزگار is the temporal phrase 'in these times' placed after the verb for poetic emphasis. آمده‌ست is the compound perfect ('has come') used to state a current/resultant state.
segments.s112-g29: بیفشارد is an archaic/poetic past form meaning 'he pressed/gripped' (here 'seized'); in this verse the verb precedes its object (V + O), which is common in epic diction. بر دست راست is a locative phrase 'on (his) right hand' (بر + noun), with دست راست 'right hand' as a compound.
segments.s112-g30: به زور جهان‌دار is an ablative/instrumental phrase 'by the might/force of the world‑ruler' where جهان‌دار specifies whose زور is meant (poetic genitive without an explicit ezafe). بر پای خاست is a verbal phrase 'rose to his feet / stood up' (بر پای = 'on foot', خاست = archaic 'rose').
segments.s112-g31: بشد here is an archaic/periphrastic past meaning roughly 'he moved/attacked' (poetic 'he set about / it came to be'); بر پی = 'after / in pursuit of' (prepositional phrase), so بشد بر پی میش = 'he set upon / pursued the ewe'. تیغش به چنگ گرفته is a participial clause 'having taken his sword into his grip' (تیغش 'his sword' with possessive suffix, به چنگ 'into the grasp').
segments.s112-g32: گرفته is the past participle 'having taken / having seized' and here scopes over the remainder: گرفته به دست دگر پالهنگ = '(having taken ... ) with the other hand (he seized) the leopard.' به دست دگر = 'with the other hand' (به as instrumental), پالهنگ the direct object 'leopard'; poetry often omits a repeated finite verb, so گرفته covers both actions.
segments.s112-g17: This clause has a nominal subject phrase ('گنه‌کار و افگندگان') followed by the verb 'تواند' used here as a modal/predicative meaning 'may/can be'. Word order is subject + predicate (typical older Persian). 'و' simply joins two coordinated nouns; 'تواند' is third-person singular past-form used in classical Persian for modal possibility.
segments.s112-g18: Parallel to the previous clause, this gives two predicates ('پرستنده و بندگان') after the same modal 'تواند' (understood as repeated): 'may be a worshipper and a servant.' The coordinating 'و' links the two role nouns; the verb form is the same modal/predicative as above.
segments.s112-g19: The noun phrase 'تن پیلوارش' ('his body, continuously/ever-suffering') is the subject; 'چنان' is an adverb meaning 'so' and modifies the following predicate 'تفته شد' ('became tifté' — a poetic/passive-stative result). The verb 'شد' is the past tense auxiliary 'became' forming a result state.
segments.s112-g20: This is a result clause introduced by the comparative/resultive construction 'چنان ... که' (so ... that); here 'که' links the degree in the first segment to its consequence. 'از تشنگی' is an ablative/cause phrase 'from/because of thirst', followed by two adjectives 'سست و آشفته' ('weak and distraught'), and the verb 'شد' again marks the resulting state 'became'.
segments.s112-g21: 'بیفتاد' is a past-tense verb meaning 'fell' (third person singular); the following proper name 'رستم' is the subject in inverted order (verb first, then subject), common in poetic narrative. The prepositional phrase 'بر آن گرم خاک' ('upon that hot earth') completes the verb's direction/locative object.
segments.s112-g22: This is a descriptive clause about Rostam's mouth: 'زبان گشته' uses the participle 'گشته' ('having become/turned') to mark a resultant state, followed by the causal phrase 'از تشنگی' ('from thirst') and the reduplicated adjective 'چاک چاک' (an expressive/poetic intensive meaning 'split, gaping, torn'). There is no new explicit verb because 'گشته' carries the participial/resultant sense.
segments.s112-g23: 'همانگه' is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that very moment'. 'یکی میش نیکوسرین' is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain/fair-faced ewe' (the adjective follows the noun, a typical adjective-noun order in classical Persian poetry). 'یکی' here functions as 'a certain' or 'one'.
segments.s112-g24: The verb 'بپیمود' is a past tense poetic verb meaning 'traversed/approached/came' (third-person singular). 'پیش' is a preposition meaning 'before/in the presence of' and takes 'تهمتن' ('the mighty one', an epithet for Rostam) as its object; 'زمین' ('ground/earth') gives the scene/grounding. Word order here is verb + prepositional phrase, normal in narrative poetry.
segments.s112-g9: چنین گفت is a standard narrative phrase 'thus said' (past tense narrative). کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the following title; داور دادگر is the addressee/epithet 'just Judge' (dāvar + adjectival دادگر). The whole segment functions as the speech-introducer: 'Thus he said, "O just Judge..."'.
segments.s112-g10: همه رنج و سختی is the noun phrase 'all suffering and hardship' (subject/topic). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun ('you') placed after the subject for emphasis/targeting; آری here functions as an emphatic/verbal particle 'indeed / will be' in an archaic/poetic sense; به سر is an idiomatic locative 'upon the head' meaning 'befall' or 'come to an end' depending on context. Together: 'All your suffering and hardship shall indeed be upon (your) head / shall come to an end.'
segments.s112-g11: گرایدونک is an archaic/poetic optative/ desiderative form meaning roughly 'may you find / may you take (a small) pleasure'. خشنودی 'pleasure' is the object of that verb; از رنج من 'from my suffering' shows the source (از + noun + possessive), so the clause reads 'may you find pleasure in / from my suffering.'
segments.s112-g12: بدان here is a fused/discourse particle 'by/with that' or 'thereby' (ب + دان in the manuscript segmentation) linking the previous clause to the command. گیتی آگنده کن is an imperative construction: آگنده 'filled' + کن 'make!' = 'make the world full'. گنج من follows as the thing with which the world is to be filled: 'fill the world with my treasure.' The word order is somewhat inverted for poetic meter (verb phrase before its object phrase).
segments.s112-g13: بپویم is an older/poetic first-person form meaning 'I go / I will go on' (progressive sense), and همی is a continuative adverb 'still/continually'. تا introduces purpose or result 'so that / until', and the following مگر here introduces a sense of possibility ('perhaps' / 'may it be that') in classical usage. کردگار is the subject that will complete the purpose clause (the Creator/God). Together they form a purpose/optative clause: 'I go on so that perhaps the Creator ...'.
segments.s112-g14: دهد is a 3rd-person verb 'gives' used here with optative force ('may he give' / 'may he grant') because of the foregoing تا مگر. شاه کاووس را uses را as the direct-object marker attached to the personal name 'King Kavus' (شاه کاووس = 'King Kavus'); زینهار is the noun 'a warning/admonition' and is the object being given. Word order places the verb before its object phrases for poetic rhythm: 'may the Creator give King Kavus a warning.'
segments.s112-g15: هم is additive 'also/and'. ایرانیان را is the definite/accusative-marked object 'the Iranians' (را marks them as the affected group). ز چنگال دیو is a prepositional phrase 'from the claw(s) of the demon' (ز = from, چنگال = claw, دیو = demon) indicating release from danger. Together: 'And free the Iranians from the demon's claws.'
segments.s112-g16: گشاید is a verbal form meaning 'may (he) open / make free / release' (optative/imperative sense). بی‌آزار 'without harm / harmless' qualifies the resulting state, and گیهان here means 'the world' (the thing to be made safe). خدیو is a vocative/title 'ruler/sovereign' addressing the divine or sovereign figure; placed at the end it functions as an apostrophe: 'O ruler of the world, make it harmless.' The order (verb + predicate + object + vocative) reflects poetic inversion.
segments.s112-g33: This is a compact, somewhat inverted locative clause: به ره = 'on the road' (به as preposition + ره 'way'), followed by بر یکی چشمه = 'upon/a certain spring' (بر + یکی 'a certain' + چشمه 'spring'). آمد پدید is the verbal phrase 'came forth/appeared' with the verb آمد ('came') and the adverbial/verb پدید 'manifest/appear' placed before it (poetic word order).
segments.s112-g34: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. میش سراور is a compound noun phrase 'the leading ewe' (میش 'ewe' + سراور 'leader/first among them'); بدانجا 'to that place' (بد + انجا) is the directional adverb, and رسید is the past-tense verb 'reached/arrived'.
segments.s112-g35: تهمتن is an epithet/name (here the hero) used as the subject. سوی آسمان = 'toward the sky' (سوی + آسمان). کرد روی literally 'made (کرد) [his] face (روی)' — a common Persian periphrasis meaning 'turned his face'. The verb کرد is past tense (he did/made).
segments.s112-g36: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین 'thus', گفت past tense 'said'). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing someone, followed by the addressee داور راستگوی 'the just/truth‑telling Judge' (compound noun + adjective). The whole segment is a direct speech introducer.
segments.s112-g37: هرانکس که ... introduces a general conditional/relative clause 'whoever who...'. از دادگر یک خدای literally 'from the just one, a God' — i.e. 'from the just God' (از indicating source/separation, دادگر 'the just one', یک خدای 'one God' used appositionally).
segments.s112-g38: بپیچد is the present/subjunctive form of پیچیدن here meaning 'turn (away)' — used as the verb of the relative clause ('whoever turns away'). نیارد is an archaic negative verb form (here equivalent to نیاورد) meaning 'does not/will not bring' or 'will not put'; خرد را 'wisdom' marked by the object marker را; به جای 'to its place' ('to the proper place'). Overall: 'will not bring wisdom to its place.'
segments.s112-g39: برین is a high-register compound of بر + این meaning 'at/on this'; چشمه آبشخور = 'spring, a watering-place' (چشمه 'spring', آبشخور 'watering-place'); میش نیست = 'there is no ewe-watering [place]' or 'it is not a watering-place for the ewes' (نیست negates existence).
segments.s112-g40: همان غرم دشتی is a nominal phrase 'that very harm/loss of the plain' (همان 'that/same', غرم 'harm/loss', دشتی 'of the plain' or 'plainly/pertaining to the plain'). مرا خویش نیست = 'is not my own' where مرا is the object pronoun 'me/my' (here written split: م + را), خویش 'own/one's own', and نیست 'is not'. The clause denies personal ownership/concern: 'it is not mine.'
segments.s112-g41: به marks direction/target ('to/into') and scopes over the following noun phrase. جایی که تنگ اندر آید سخن is a relative clause: جایی 'place' + که introducing 'where', تنگ 'narrow/constrained', اندر آید 'comes/occurs within' and سخن 'speech' (placed at the end for poetic word order). Together: 'to a place where speech becomes constrained.'
segments.s112-g42: پناهت is 'your refuge' (پناه + pronominal -ت). The clause is negative imperative: مکن 'do not make/do' (command). به جز = 'except to / but' (جز gives the exception). پاک یزدان is the object 'the pure God.' Word order is object/phrase before the negative imperative, a poetic inversion: 'Do not make your refuge to anyone except the pure God.'
segments.s112-g43: بران is a fused/poetic 'بر' + 'آن' meaning 'upon/for that'. غرم = 'my/that wrath' (object/theme). بر آفرین کرد = 'made/uttered acclamations/praises' (آفرین here = praise/acclamation, کرد = did/made). چند at the end here means 'several / many (times)' rather than a question. Overall this is a compact, somewhat elliptical clause: 'Upon that wrath he made many acclamations.'
segments.s112-g44: که introduces the following wish/clause explaining purpose or result. از چرخ گردان is a source phrase 'from the revolving sky/heavens' (چرخ = heavens/fate, گردان = revolving). مبادت is the negative optative 'may it not be' (poetic wish), and گزند 'harm' is the thing wished against. Word order places the wished-against noun (گزند) at the end: 'that no harm may come from the turning heavens.'
segments.s112-g45: گیا 'vegetation/plant life' is the subject; بر در و دشت تو is a locative phrase 'on/at your door and plain' (بر = on/upon, در = door/gate here, دشت = plain). سبز is the predicate adjective 'green/verdant' and باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (classical/poetic use). The whole clause is a wish: 'May plants be green on your door and plain.'
segments.s112-g46: مباد is the negative optative 'may it not be'. از تو 'because of/from you' gives the cause; هرگز 'never' negates in time; دل یوز is a possessive/genitive phrase 'the heart of the hunting animal (yūz)' and شاد is the predicate 'be glad'. This is an elliptical optative clause: 'May the heart of the hunting animal never be glad because of you.'
segments.s112-g47: ترا (split here as ت + را in the tokens) is the definite object 'you' (را marks the definite object). هرک is an indefinite 'whoever/whichever'. یازد is the verb 'strikes/attacks' (here used for 'shoots/strikes at'); word order places the object before the verb (poetic). به ... تیر و کمان is an instrumental phrase 'with arrow and bow' where به functions as 'by/with'. Overall: 'Whoever strikes you with arrow and bow...' (the event described hypothetically).
segments.s112-g48: This is a pair of optative/jussive clauses describing the wished curse. شکسته کمان (adjective before noun) = 'broken bow', followed by باد 'may it be' (optative). The second conjunct 'و تیره گمان' is elliptical: 'and (may there be) dark/gloomy thought(s)' — the verb is omitted but understood from the previous optative. The structure yields: 'may his bow be broken and (may he have) dark thoughts.'
segments.s112-g49: که is the relative/complementizer 'that/who' introducing this subordinate clause. زنده شد is a past tense/perfect-like phrase 'became alive' (زدنده + شد = 'was revived/became alive'). از marks the agent/ablative 'by/from you' (از + تو = 'by you'), and گو پیلتن functions here as a vocative or proper name addressed after از تو (treated as the addressee: 'O Go‑Piltan').
segments.s112-g50: وگرنه is a connective meaning 'otherwise' introducing the counterfactual consequence. پراندیشه is an adjectival/compound word meaning 'full of care/worry' (here implying the condition of the dead/shrouded), بود is the past copula 'was/would have been', and از کفن uses از with کفن 'shroud' to indicate involvement/condition (i.e. 'in/with a shroud' — figuratively 'would have been dead and shrouded').
segments.s112-g51: که again introduces a relative clause 'that/which'. در + سینه is the prepositional phrase 'in the chest/inside the breast'. اژدهای بزرگ is a noun phrase 'the great dragon' (اژدهای = dragon, بزرگ = large/great) serving as the locative possessor of the chest.
segments.s112-g52: نگنجد is the negative-present/subjunctive sense of گنجیدن 'to fit/be contained' (here 'does not fit' or 'should not fit' in the conditional pattern). بماند is the verb 'remain' (third person), used here as the result clause: 'will remain'. به + چنگال گرگ indicates the location/recipient 'in/to the talon/claw of the wolf' (به here = 'into/in'), so the clause reads 'if it doesn't fit into the dragon's chest, it will remain in the wolf's claw.'
segments.s112-g53: شده is the perfect/passive-like auxiliary 'has become/was', forming a participial clause. پاره‌پاره is an adverb/adjectival phrase 'in pieces', and کنان and کشان are adverbial participles formed with the -ان ending ('doing so' / 'thus' and 'dragging' respectively), describing the manner: 'having been torn to pieces and dragged.'
segments.s112-g54: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from' (agent/source). رستم is the proper name Rostam (agent/source reference). به دشمن reads 'to the enemy' (به = to/toward), رسیده is a perfect/participial adjective 'arrived' or 'reached', and نشان is a noun 'sign/token/mark' — together: 'arrived as a token from Rostam to the enemy' (i.e. brought as a trophy/mark).
segments.s112-g55: روانش is 'his soul/spirit' (روان + possessive -ش). چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. پردخته شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'was loosed/separated/put in a composed state' (here 'was released' or 'was taken away'); together 'when his spirit was released'. ز آفرین uses ز with آفرین (here read as 'by the Creator' / 'by Aferin' — i.e. indicating the agent/source of that release).
segments.s112-g56: ز + رخش indicates 'from his steed/horse' (ز = from, رخش = steed). تگاور is 'the rider/warrior' (here identifying whose steed or the actor). جدا کرد is a transitive verbal phrase 'separated/removed' (جدا = apart, کرد = did/made), and زین is 'the saddle' (object). The clause therefore describes removing the saddle from the horse.

sentences.s112-s37: He slept and was at rest, and did not open his lips; he kept Rakhsh grazing until midnight.
sentences.s112-s1: A certain path came before him; inevitable, he went and had to stop, standing there staring at the sight.
sentences.s112-s2: After the horse — and, it seemed, the rider's tongue — from the heat and from thirst he became out of action (was exhausted).
sentences.s112-s3: He dismounted from the horse and, holding his gambeson in his hand, went on, moving like a drunk.
sentences.s112-s4: He kept searching for a remedy, and then he turned his face toward the sky.
sentences.s112-s29: He washed his whole body with that pure water; he became radiant like the sun.
sentences.s112-s30: When he was sated, he readied his hunting gear; he girded his waist and filled his quiver with arrows.
sentences.s112-s31: He hurled him down like an elephant; he stripped from him the hide of the legs and the belly.
sentences.s112-s32: Like the sun he kindled a sharp fire; he lifted it from the water and burned it in the flames.
sentences.s112-s33: It leapt from the fire and began to be eaten; its bones began to be consigned to the earth.
sentences.s112-s34: He came toward the bright spring to the water; when he was sated he made for sleep.
sentences.s112-s35: Tahmten spoke to his Rakhsh, the singer: 'Do not strive with anyone, and do not become a mate either.'
sentences.s112-s36: If an enemy comes, make for me; do not, you, go into battle with demons and lions.
sentences.s112-s13: At that movement a thought arose in the ewe; she said to herself, "Where is this watering-place?"
sentences.s112-s14: Truly, the generosity of the Creator has risen on high in these times.
sentences.s112-s15: He gripped the sword in his right hand, and by the might of the world‑ruler he rose to his feet.
sentences.s112-s16: He sprang in pursuit of the ewe, and, having taken his sword in his grasp, with his other hand he seized the leopard.
sentences.s112-s9: The sinner and the wrongdoers may be worshippers and servants.
sentences.s112-s10: His continually-suffering body became so worn that, from thirst, he grew weak and distraught.
sentences.s112-s11: Rostam fell upon that hot earth; his tongue, turned by thirst, was split and torn.
sentences.s112-s12: At that moment a fair-faced ewe came forward before the mighty Tahmatan on the ground.
sentences.s112-s5: Thus he said: O just Judge, all your suffering and hardship shall indeed come to an end.
sentences.s112-s6: May you take delight in my suffering; thereby fill the world with my treasure.
sentences.s112-s7: I will go on so that perhaps the Creator may give King Kavus a warning.
sentences.s112-s8: And (may he) free the Iranians from the demon's claws; O sovereign of the world, make it harmless.
sentences.s112-s17: On the road a certain spring appeared; when the leading ewe reached that place.
sentences.s112-s18: Tahmten turned his face toward the sky and thus said, 'O just and truthful Judge!'
sentences.s112-s19: Whoever turns away from the just God will not restore wisdom to its proper place.
sentences.s112-s20: At this spring there is no watering-place for the ewes; that very harm/loss of the plain is not mine.
sentences.s112-s21: When speech comes into a tight spot, take refuge in none but the pure God.
sentences.s112-s22: He raised that wrath and made acclamations several times, that no harm might come from the revolving heavens.
sentences.s112-s23: May vegetation on your doorstep and plains be green; may the heart of the hunting animal never be glad because of you.
sentences.s112-s24: May whoever strikes at you with arrow and bow have his bow broken and be filled with gloomy thoughts.
sentences.s112-s25: who was brought back to life by you, O Go-Piltan; otherwise he would have been shrouded (i.e. dead).
sentences.s112-s26: that, if it does not fit in the chest of the great dragon, will remain in the wolf’s talon.
sentences.s112-s27: having been torn to pieces and dragged, it came from Rostam to the enemy as a trophy/mark.
sentences.s112-s28: When his spirit was loosed by the Creator, he removed the saddle from his steed.
